title:
ResponsiveView
Extension
subtitle: A VS Code extension that makes responsive testing part of every build — not an afterthought.

slides.title:
ResponsiveView
Extension
slides.description: In my vibe coding course, students shipped apps that broke on every device but their own. I designed and built a VS Code extension using Claude Code to fix that — zero context-switch, real device frames, right inside the editor.

metaItems.label: Tools
metaItems.value: TypeScript · CSS · VS Code API · Claude Code

image.caption: [ADD: hero screenshot — the extension panel open in VS Code with a device frame visible]

slides.label: Built With AI
slides.title: Designed and built with Claude Code
slides.content: Not just designing a tool — shipping one. I built the whole extension with Claude Code: TypeScript, the VS Code API, the UI. Idea to working extension, no engineering handoff.
issues: I wrote the prompts, reviewed the output, owned every design decision
Claude Code handled implementation; I handled judgment
[ADD: a Claude Code session or the extension running]
slides.conclusion: I teach building with AI — and built this with it.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: Why students skipped responsive testing
slides.description: Apps shipped fine on the student's screen — broken everywhere else. Four friction points, one root cause.

issues.title: One-screen tunnel vision
issues.description: Built and tested on their own monitor — never checked other viewports until something broke.

issues.title: DevTools felt technical
issues.description: DevTools device mode is powerful but intimidating — the mental overhead made students avoid it.

issues.title: Window-switching broke focus
issues.description: Switching between editor and browser broke the coding flow. Most students just skipped the check.

issues.title: No real device frame
issues.description: A pixel-width number doesn't feel like a phone. Without a real frame, the size didn't stick.

slides.highlight: Remove the switching cost and you remove the adoption problem. The barrier wasn't knowledge — it was friction.

slides.label: Before & After
slides.title: From tab-switching to one panel
slides.description: Old workflow: five steps to a number. New: one click to a frame.
slides.beforeDescription: Left the editor, opened DevTools, typed a viewport width, guessed at a device — then switched back to code.
slides.afterDescription: One click to the panel, pick a device, see a real frame — still inside the editor.
beforeBullets: 3–5 steps to reach a responsive preview
No visual device context — just numbers
afterBullets: One-click access to any device size
CSS device frames with notches and stands
Preview stays open while code is edited
slides.highlight: [ADD: any measured improvement — e.g. adoption rate, student feedback, time-to-test reduction]

slides.label: The Solution
slides.title: A preview panel that lives inside the editor
slides.content: ResponsiveView opens as a VS Code panel. Enter a localhost URL, pick a device, and instantly see the app inside a realistic frame. I kept the surface minimal: device selection, orientation, theme, and refresh — nothing to learn.
issues: URL input remembers the last address between sessions
Device picker grouped by category: phones, tablets, laptops, desktops, ultrawide
Auto-scaling preview fits any panel size without scrolling

slides.label: Reflection
slides.title: What building this taught me
whatWorked: Placement was the strategy — it was already in the editor.
CSS frames made the size feel real. Students took checks more seriously.
whatFailed: [ADD: anything that didn't go as planned — e.g. an early UI approach that confused students, a technical constraint that forced a design compromise, features that were cut]
whatYoudDoDifferently: I'd have built side-by-side from day one. Students kept asking for both views.
slides.whatYouLearned: The goal wasn't completeness — it was changing a habit. Friction is a design decision.
slides.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Cleaner demos were visible. Long-term habit change is harder to trace.
